rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO POLICE OFFICERS' ASSOCIATION VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO AMENDED PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO AMENDED PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION.(part 1 of 2) CCSF's demurrer to Plaintiff Lawrence Kempinski's first amended verified petition for writ of mandate for violation of Gov't Code § 3304(b) is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 430.10(e).) For purposes of this demurrer, the court will assume that the Chief's decision to file a disciplinary complaint against officer Kempinski is "punitive action" within the meaning of Gov't Code § 3304(b). (See Morgado v. CCSF (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 1, 8.) Nevertheless, the allegations and judicially noticeable documents show that the Police Commission provided a comprehensive quasi-judicial hearing process to determine whether the Chief's complaint had merit and whether officer Kempinski should be disciplined. As the Court of Appeal observed in Morgado, 13 Cal.App.5th at 13, "[The Commission] provides an opportunity for an officer to develop a formal record of the underlying events and other circumstances surrounding the City's disciplinary decision, and the proceeding is an evidentiary hearing before a neutral fact finder, i.e., the Commission is not 'the individual or agency that has initiated punitive action against a peace officer' [citation.].") Officer Kempinski retired before that process could be completed. (Amended Petition, 1.) As petitioner concedes, it is only the Commission-not the Chief of Police or the hearing officer-that could terminate officer Kempinski (Amended Petition, 2, 12-13) and the Commission had the discretion to entertain additional evidence before making its final decision. (Charter, § A8.343; Rule XII(B)(3).) Officer Kempinski retired before the Commission had a chance to hold a hearing or make a final decision. The general rule is that PSOPBRA appeal rights come into existence only after a final "decision." (See Doyle v. City of Chino (1981) 117 Cal.App.3d 673, 678-679.) It is quite possible that the Commission could have rejected the Chief's disciplinary complaint and/or ordered further evidence to be presented. In addition, as previously noted by Judge Kahn, officer Kempinski could have sought pre-hearing relief to vindicate his PSOPBRA rights with regard to the lack of writ relief or other pre-decision structural problems with the Police Commission's hearing process. (See Gov't Code § 3309.5; Alameida v. State Personnel Bd. (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 46, 54; Mounger v. Gates (1987) 193 Cal.App.3d 1248, 1256-1257.) Officer Kempinski chose neither of these avenues to vindicate his rights. Officer Kempinski had "an opportunity for administrative appeal" before the Commission within the meaning of Gov't Code § 3304(b) and therefore, there was no violation of that provision and his first cause of action fails to state a claim. Officer Kempinski's new allegations regarding the hearing officer's conduct and statements and legal counsel's statement that officer Kempinski would be placed on unpaid leave do not show that invocation of the Commission proceedings would have been futile. "The futility exception requires that the party invoking the exception 'can positively state that the [agency] has declared what its ruling will be on a particular case.' " (Jonathan Neil & Assoc., Inc. v. Jones (2004) 33 Cal.4th 917, 936 [rejecting claim of futility as to primary jurisdiction of Department of Insurance where "[t]here is nothing in the record to indicate how the DOI would have decided the [insureds'] case had the latter fully availed themselves of the . . . complaint and appeals (part 1 of 2 )=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO POLICE OFFICERS' ASSOCIATION VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Demurrer To Amended Petition For Writ Of Mandate/ Prohibition/ Certification. (Part 2 Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 1, Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Demurrer To Amended Petition For Writ Of Mandate/ Prohibition/ Certification. (Part 2 Of 2 Tentative Ruling Only) processes."]; see also Coachella Valley Mosquito & Vector Control Dist. v. California Public Employment Relations Bd. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1072, 1081 ["exhaustion doctrine 'requires that a party must not only initially raise the issue in the administrative forum, but he must proceed through the entire proceeding to a final decision on the merits of the entire controversy.'"].) The Commission could have disagreed with the hearing officer's recommendation or, as discussed above, rejected the Chief's disciplinary complaint. Petitioner has not shown that further administrative proceedings before the Commission would have been futile because the outcome of those proceedings was known in advance. Officer Kempinski's claim of financial duress does not show futility. Officer Kempinski was entitled to a prompt hearing if he were put on unpaid administrative leave. (Charter A8.344; Departmental General Order 2.07(C)(10)-(11).) Gov't Code § 3304(b) does not guarantee an appeal before punitive action is taken. (See Browning v. Block (1985) 175 Cal.App.3d 423, 428; Butler v. County of Los Angeles (1981) 116 Cal.App.3d 633, 639.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for respondent City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LUMETA, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. WUXI SUNTECH POWER CO., LTD., A CHINESE CORPORATIO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT WUXI SUNTECH POWER CO., LTD's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND.(part 1 of 2) Defendant Wuxi Suntech Power Co., Ltd.'s motion to set aside default and vacate default judgement pursuant to CCP § 473.5(a) is granted. Since any doubts as to the application of section 437 are resolved in favor of the party seeking relief, the court finds that defendant has shown it did not receive actual notice in time to defend and that lack of notice was not due to defendant's avoidance of service or excusable neglect. The declaration of Mr. Jiang states that no papers were received by Wuxi until the local court served process pursuant to the Hague Convention, and that fact is corroborated by plaintiff's own declaration that previous attempts to serve by mail failed and the mail was returned undelivered. (Jiang Decl. 5,6; Luskin Decl. 3, Exh. A) (See Ramos v. Homeward Residential, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1434, 1444 ["when a party in default moves promptly to seek relief, very slight evidence is required to justify a trial court's order setting aside a default. . . Even in a case where the showing ... is not strong, or where there is any doubt as to setting aside of a default, such doubt should be resolved in favor of the application."].) Imputed notice or constructive notice is not "actual" notice. (Rosenthal v. Garner (1983) 142 Cal.App.3d 891, 895.) Thus, Wuxi's constructive notice of the lawsuit through the bankruptcy proceeding did not constitute actual notice. (See Olvera v. Olvera (1991) 232 Cal.App.3d 32, 41 [defendant's awareness that lawsuit had been filed, based on receipt of a notice of recordation of lis pendens, insufficient].) Unlike Tunis v. Barrow (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d 1069, where the corporate defendants' declarations in support of the motion to vacate default judgment "fail[ed] to show that the corporate defendants did not receive such notice from the secretary of state," (id. at 1079), here Mr. Jiang's declaration establishes that Wuxi did not receive actual notice of the lawsuit or the default judgment until November 19, 2018. (Jiang Decl. 6.) Pulte Homes Corp. v. Williams Mechanical, Inc. (2016) 2 Cal.App.5th 267 does not stand for the broad proposition that service through the Secretary of State per Corp. Code, § 2111 resulted in actual notice as a matter of law. The holding in Pulte was limited to the circumstances of the case, where the dissolved corporation had designated an agent for service of process who received actual notice of the action. (Pulte Homes Corp., 2 Cal.App.5th at 273-274 ["a corporation can have actual notice only through an agent who has the appropriate authority."].)(part 1 of 2)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LUMETA, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. WUXI SUNTECH POWER CO., LTD., A CHINESE CORPORATIO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Wuxi Suntech Power Co., Ltd'S Motion To Set Aside Default/Default Judgment/Leave To Defend.(Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 2, Defendant Wuxi Suntech Power Co., Ltd'S Motion To Set Aside Default/Default Judgment/Leave To Defend.(Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only) Unless inexcusable neglect is clear, the policy favoring trial on the merits prevails. Doubts are resolved in favor of the application for relief from default. (Tunis v. Barrow (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d at 1079.) Here, neither excusable neglect nor avoidance of service is clear from the evidence. Plaintiff's arguments to the contrary require a number of inferential leaps to be resolved in its favor, when the law dictates that doubts be resolved in favor of the party seeking to litigate on the merits. The motion is timely because defendant filed its motion less than two years from the entry of the default judgment. (Jiang Decl. 5,6.) The products were installed in 2011 and default was entered at the end of 2017, thus any prejudice to plaintiff resulting from setting aside the default approximately one year later is not so substantial as to outweigh defendant's interest in defending the case on the merits, nor has plaintiff shown that it will be unable to locate material witnesses to which it had access at the time the default judgment was entered. The default and default judgment shall be vacated and defendant shall file its answer to the complaint within five days of the entry of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(part 2 of 2)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BUENOFRIO INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL VS. NIESAR & VESTAL LLP A LIMITED PARTNERSHIP ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Second Motion To Seal Lodged Papers Containing Confidential Information And Trade Secrets
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT GERALD NIESAR, OSCAR ESCOBAR, NIESAR & VESTAL, LLP's Second Motion To Seal Lodged Papers Containing Confidential Information And Trade Secrets. Defendants Niesar & Vestal LLP, Gerald Niesar, and Oscar Escobar's second motion to seal lodged containing confidential information and trade secrets is granted. No opposition filed, and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PAUL O'GRADY VS. MATTHEW CONWAY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Matthew Conway, Joshua Mcadam, Anthony Healy-London, Burgers 355 Llc Dba Brass Tacks' Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.(Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 7, Defendant Matthew Conway, Joshua Mcadam, Anthony Healy-London, Burgers 355 Llc Dba Brass Tacks' Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.(Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only) Plaintiff's other evidence is also flawed. The O'Grady declaration was filed late and the Court likewise declines to consider it. Even if the Court were to consider the declaration, it contains inadmissible hearsay statements by Ryan Gilbert and an unnamed bar customer or employee. (O'Grady Decl. 4, 7.) The Gilbert declaration presented by Plaintiff in his late-filed opposition papers has no evidentiary value as it is not signed. (Code Civ. Proc. § 2015.5; see People v. Bryant (2011) 191 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1467-1469 [unsworn affidavits were not admissible].) Plaintiff's opposition also badly distorts the deposition testimony of Ms. Keating by juxtaposing a question from page 10 with an answer to a different question on page 21. Finally, Plaintiff presents no evidence of later instances of publication of defamatory statements to contradict Mr. Conway's sworn declaration. (Conway Decl. 3.) Because Plaintiff has failed to make any evidentiary showing that Mr. Conway or the other named Defendants made defamatory statements about Plaintiff, he is unable to satisfy an element of his first two causes of action for slander per se. (Civ. Code §46 [slander per se requires "false and unprivileged publication" charging a person with a crime].) Plaintiff's attempts to impeach Ms. Keating's credibility because of her health condition are not grounds for denying this motion. Summary judgment may not be denied on "grounds of credibility or for want of cross-examination of witnesses furnishing affidavits or declarations in support of summary judgment." (Code Civ. Proc. §437c(e).) The other three causes of action for intentional and negligent infliction of emotional distress and for negligence are derivative of the slander per se claims and are based on the same alleged facts. Therefore, all of Plaintiff's claims fail and Defendants are entitled to summary judgment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested (end of part 2 of 2)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PAUL O'GRADY VS. MATTHEW CONWAY
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT MATTHEW CONWAY, JOSHUA MCADAM, ANTHONY HEALY-LONDON, BURGERS 355 LLC DBA BRASS TACKS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.(part 1 of 2) Defendants Matthew Conway, Joshua McAdam, Anthony Healy-London, and Burgers 355 LLC dba Brass Tacks' motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendants' moving papers showed that Mr. Conway did not make any slanderous publication about Plaintiff Paul O'Grady drugging a female patron's drink at the Brass Tacks bar. Mr. Conway's Declaration, Kimberly Keating's deposition testimony, and Ryan Gilbert's Declaration included with the moving papers establish that Mr. Conway did not make the alleged defamatory statements about Plaintiff. As described by Ms. Keating, the statements by Mr. Conway to Ms. Keating explained that the female patron-Mr. Conway's girlfriend-said Plaintiff drugged her, not Mr. Conway. (Price Decl. 3, Exh. B 10:16-11:2.) The Gilbert Declaration establishes that Mr. Conway never made any defamatory statements to him about Plaintiff. The burden shifted to Plaintiff but he presented no admissible evidence to create a dispute of material fact. The Court sustains Defendants' evidentiary objections, as follows. The investigator's memorandum included with Plaintiff's opposition is inadmissible hearsay under Evidence Code section 1200 as it is the investigator's statement containing the statements of nonparty witnesses offered to prove the truth of the matters asserted. (See People v. Sundlee (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 477, 483 [holding that electronic voice recording of nonparty surveillance team monitoring defendant suspected of illegally setting fires was inadmissible hearsay].) The past recollection recorded exception of Evidence Code section 1237 is inapplicable. Further, the memorandum is not properly authenticated, as it was attached to the declaration of Plaintiff's attorney, who lacks personal knowledge or foundation to support the veracity of the memo's contents. (See Evid. Code §§ 403 [foundation], 702 [personal knowledge].) Plaintiff's attempt to authenticate the memo was belatedly made in the late-filed Andrew Paul Kangas Declaration, which was filed on March 15, 2019, after the reply papers were received by the Court and only six days before the date of the scheduled hearing. The Court declines to consider those late-filed papers. (See Cal. R. Ct. 3.1300(d) [court, in its discretion, may refuse to consider late filed papers].)(part 1 of 2)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JERRY H. KU VS. ANNA KU WONG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Jerry Ku Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendants Anna Ku Wong, Rena Ku Chin, Ava Ku, Beulah Ku, Harold Fong, Harry Fong And Melinda Low To Plaintiff Jerry H. Ku Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One (Tenative Ruling Only Part 2 0f 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, March 21, 2019, Line 2, Plaintiff Jerry Ku's Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendants Anna Ku Wong, Rena Ku Chin, Ava Ku, Beulah Ku, Harold Fong, Harry Fong And Melinda Low To Plaintiff Jerry H. Ku Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One (Tenative Ruling Part 2 0f 2) A court may not order production of materials claimed to be privileged without permitting the opportunity to support the claim. Catalina Island Yacht Club v. Super. Ct., 242 Cal. App. 4th 1116, 1131 (2015). Because Defendants' objections are based on a claim of privilege, Defendants are required to provide a privilege log that will allow Plaintiff - and the Court if necessary - to determine whether the communications qualify for a privilege, and then whether any disclosures were made that would operate to waive privilege. "[A] privilege log typically should provide the identity and capacity of all individuals who authored, sent, or received each allegedly privileged document, the document's date, a brief description of the document and its contents or subject matter sufficient to determine whether the privilege applies, and the precise privilege or protection asserted." Id. at 1130; see also Code of Civ. Pro. § 2031.240(b)(2), (c)(1). Any materials that, on review by Defendants, do not qualify for privilege must be produced. The request for sanctions was not made in the notice of motion and is therefore DENIED. Code of Civ. Pro. § 2023.040. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tentative ruling Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JERRY H. KU VS. ANNA KU WONG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendants Anna Ku Wong, Rena Ku Chin, Ava Ku, Beulah Ku, Harold Fong, Harry Fong And Melinda Low To Plaintiff Jerry H. Ku Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, March 21, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF JERRY KU'S Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendants Anna Ku Wong, Rena Ku Chin, Ava Ku, Beulah Ku, Harold Fong, Harry Fong And Melinda Low To Plaintiff Jerry H. Ku Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One. (tenative ruling part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is GRANTED IN PART - Defendants are ordered to produce a privilege log. "The 'joint defense privilege' and the 'common interest privilege' have not been recognized by statute in California." OXY Resources Cal. LLC v. Super. Ct., 115 Cal. App. 4th 874, 889 (2004). "Therefore, a party seeking to rely on the common interest doctrine does not satisfy its burden to justify a claim of privilege simply by demonstrating that a confidential communication took place between parties who purportedly share a common interest. Rather, the party seeking to invoke the doctrine must first establish that the communicated information would otherwise be protected from disclosure by a claim of privilege." Id. at 890. "The next step in the analysis is to determine whether disclosing the information to a party outside the attorney-client relationship waived any applicable privileges." Id. ; see also Behunin v. Super. Ct., 9 Cal. App. 5th 833, 853 (2017) (discussing common interest doctrine). (end of tenative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TAMAYA GARCIA VS. PIAO HONGMIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants Responses To Plaintiff Tamaya Garcias Special Interrogatories, Set One, Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Form Interrogatories, Set One And For An Award Of Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, March 21, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF TAMAYA GARCIA'S Motion To Compel Defendants Responses To Plaintiff Tamaya Garcias Special Interrogatories, Set One, Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Form Interrogatories, Set One And For An Award Of Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Based on the Notice of Non-Receipt dated March 13, 2019, it appears that Defendants provided responses to the subject discovery requests, albeit after they were due. Therefore the motion to compel responses to Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, and Requests for Production is DENIED AS MOOT. With respect to the request for sanctions, CRC 3.1348(a) permits an award of sanctions even if discovery responses are provided after a motion to compel is filed, and CRC 3.1348(b) provides that failure to oppose a motion does not operate as a concession of the propriety of a sanction. The meet-and-confer correspondence indicates late discovery of issues and some effort to resolve the dispute without court intervention. Nevertheless Defendants did not provide responses until after their requested extension. Balancing these considerations, Defendants are ordered to pay $1,200 to partially compensate Plaintiff for the work required to bring this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DARA AUYEUNG VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCSICO's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Judge Schulman discloses that he previously represented The Regents of the University of California. This will not affect Judge Schulman's ability to be fair and impartial in deciding this case. The demurrer of defendant City and County of San Francisco (erroneously sued as Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital) is sustained with 20 days' leave to amend. Plaintiff has leave to name the City as the proper party to this case rather than the hospital. (S.F. Charter Article 1, § 1.101; Article IV, § 4.110; S.F. Health Code, § 111.) The court rejects the City's remaining arguments. The City has failed to show that it is prohibited by statute or ordinance from entering into an enforceable employment agreement. (See Retired Employees Assoc. of Orange County v. County of Orange (2011) 52 Cal.4th 1171, 1176, 1182-1183.) The City Charter exempts from the civil service system certain "physicians and dentists serving in their professional capacity," and the City has not shown that a professional optometrist such as Plaintiff does not constitute a "physician" within the meaning of this provision. (Charter Article X, § 10.104 (13); see Kreutzer v. City and County of San Francisco (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 306, 309-310 [licensed physician who was board certified in psychiatry was hired by City into a position expressly classified by City Service Commission as exempt from civil service system].) Plaintiff's allegations naming the Hospital (the City) as an employer are not a sham. Plaintiff has consistently named the City as a party to the employment relationship since filing the original complaint. At a minimum, the allegations present a factual issue regarding who the actual employer is and/or whether a joint employment relationship exists. Unlike Owens v. Kings Supermarket (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 379, 384, plaintiff's allegations in this case are not contradictory; she has consistently alleged that the City was involved in her employment. Lastly, plaintiff sufficiently alleges that the City failed to hire her because of her gender. The FEHA claim is thus well-stated. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER Z. SILVA VS. CINDY ANN PARDINI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thurdsday, March 21, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANT CINDI PARDINI DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Pardini's demurrer is sustained with 20 days' leave to amend. Plaintiff has leave to plead a claim for slander and/or libel that (1) occurred within a year of the filing of the instant complaint on September 28, 2018 (see Code Civ. Proc. § 340(c)); and (2) does not involve privileged conduct such as a police report or statements made in a judicial proceeding. (See Civ. Code § 47; Hagberg v. California Federal Bank (2004) 32 Cal.4th 39.) Plaintiff shall remove the allegations regarding time-barred or privileged conduct. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)
